subject: AP Computer Science Principles
theme: The Internet is a decentralized, packet-switched network built on open protocols that make it scalable and fault tolerant — and parallel computing dramatically speeds up complex tasks.
core_concepts: The Internet is a global network of networks — it is decentralized (no single point of control) and packet-switched (data is broken into packets that independently route to their destination).
TCP guarantees reliable, ordered delivery of all packets (used for email, web pages); UDP is faster but does not guarantee delivery (used for video streaming, gaming).
DNS translates human-readable domain names into IP addresses. IPv4 uses 32-bit addresses (~4.3 billion); IPv6 uses 128-bit addresses (vastly more capacity).
Fault tolerance means the network can continue functioning even if some nodes fail — achieved through redundancy (multiple paths between any two points).
Parallel computing assigns different parts of a task to multiple processors working simultaneously — it is faster than sequential computing for problems that can be divided.
Distributed computing uses many separate computers on a network to solve one large problem — combines the processing power of many machines.
Open standards (publicly documented protocols) allow any manufacturer or developer to build compatible devices and software, which is why the Internet can connect billions of different devices.

key_terms.definition: A global network of interconnected networks that use agreed-upon protocols to communicate.
A collection of web pages and resources connected by hyperlinks, accessed via the Internet using HTTP/HTTPS.
A small unit of data that is transmitted independently over a network; large messages are broken into many packets.
A unique numerical label identifying a device on a network — IPv4 (32-bit) or IPv6 (128-bit).
Transmission Control Protocol — ensures all packets are delivered reliably and in the correct order.
User Datagram Protocol — sends packets without guaranteeing delivery or order; lower latency than TCP.
Domain Name System — translates domain names (google.com) into IP addresses.
Protocols for transferring web pages; HTTPS adds encryption for secure communication.
A device that forwards packets between networks, determining the optimal path for each packet.
The maximum rate of data transfer over a network connection, measured in bits per second.
The delay between sending data and receiving it — the time it takes for data to travel from source to destination.
A network's ability to continue operating correctly even when some components fail.
Having duplicate components or multiple paths so the system continues working if one part fails.
Multiple processors working on different parts of a problem simultaneously to speed up total execution.
Using multiple networked computers to collectively solve a single large problem.

exam_tip: The AP exam tests whether you can read a network diagram and determine if a network is fault tolerant — look for whether every node has at least two paths to reach any other node; also be able to calculate parallel computing speedup.
